name: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
essence: Laureate gravity, mythic air, and the long English line at full strength.
bio: Alfred Tennyson carried Victorian poetry into its public height: sonorous blank verse, Arthurian tableaux, and compressed lyrics that still feel like carved stone. Poet Laureate for over forty years, he shaped how a nation heard grief, doubt, and endurance.

works.title: The Eagle
works.subtitle: He clasps the crag with crooked hands; Close…

works.title: The Eagle
works.subtitle: He clasps the crag with crooked hands; Close…

works.title: The Eagle
works.subtitle: He clasps the crag with crooked hands; Close…

resonance: Tennyson’s lines teach a kind of listening—slow, weighted, willing to stay inside a single image until it turns.
quote: I am a part of all that I have met.

name: Bhagavad Gita
essence: A battlefield counsel on duty, devotion, and the self that moves beneath appearances.
bio: The Bhagavad Gita is a philosophical dialogue set within the epic Mahabharata: the warrior Arjuna falters, and Krishna unfolds teachings on action without clinging, knowledge, and love. Its verses have guided readers across centuries and languages as a mirror for conscience and calm.

works.title: The Warrior Who Trembled
works.subtitle: My bow slips. My mouth is dry.…

works.title: Dust Before the Signal
works.subtitle: Before the first arrow, there is only dust—…

works.title: As Is Faith, So Is the Life
works.subtitle: You become what you bow to…

resonance: Versery presents modern English renderings of this voice—meant as literary companions, not substitutes for study with teachers or traditions you trust.
quote: On this path effort never goes to waste, and there is no failure.

name: Carl Sandburg
essence: Fog-quiet observation, industrial America, and plain speech with a camera’s patience.
bio: Carl Sandburg wrote across genres—poetry, biography, journalism—yet his poems stay close to the grain of American speech: trains, cities, labor, and weather held in short, luminous strokes.

works.title: Fog
works.subtitle: The fog comes on little cat feet.…

works.title: Fog
works.subtitle: The fog comes on little cat feet.…

works.title: Fog
works.subtitle: The fog comes on little cat feet.…

resonance: His miniatures reward slow reading; a single metaphor does the work of a whole scene.
quote: The fog comes / on little cat feet.

name: Claude McKay
essence: Defiance forged into sonnets, exile, and the ache of belonging refused and demanded.
bio: Claude McKay brought Jamaican rhythm into American political verse. His work helped define the Harlem Renaissance’s moral voltage: pride under pressure, beauty beside rage.

works.title: If We Must Die
works.subtitle: If we must die—let it not be like…

works.title: If We Must Die
works.subtitle: If we must die—let it not be like…

works.title: If We Must Die
works.subtitle: If we must die—let it not be like…

resonance: McKay reminds us that formal mastery can carry protest without softening its edge.
quote: If we must die—let it not be like hogs / Hunted and penned in an inglorious spot.

name: Countee Cullen
essence: Elegant quatrains meeting racial wound and spiritual argument without flinching.
bio: Countee Cullen was a central lyric voice of the Harlem Renaissance: formal, musical, and unafraid to place Black experience inside the wider house of English poetic forms—then question that house itself.

works.title: Yet Do I Marvel
works.subtitle: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind,…

works.title: Yet Do I Marvel
works.subtitle: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind,…

works.title: Yet Do I Marvel
works.subtitle: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind,…

resonance: His poems stage doubt as cleanly as prayer—wondering what justice means when the world’s grammar feels rigged.
quote: Yet do I marvel at this curious thing: / To make a poet black, and bid him sing!

name: Edna St. Vincent Millay
essence: Sonnets with a razor’s wit—desire, independence, and grief in high polish.
bio: Edna St. Vincent Millay became a celebrity of American poetry for lines that could glitter and cut in the same breath. She worked sonnets like a playwright works scenes: tight, dramatic, unforgettable.

works.title: First Fig
works.subtitle: My candle burns at both ends; It will…

works.title: First Fig
works.subtitle: My candle burns at both ends; It will…

works.title: First Fig
works.subtitle: My candle burns at both ends; It will…

resonance: Millay’s archive presence is all voltage controlled—feeling measured so precisely it burns hotter.
quote: My candle burns at both ends; / It will not last the night.

name: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
essence: Romantic argument in ottava rima and sonnet—politics, love, and spiritual courage intertwined.
bio: Elizabeth Barrett Browning helped redefine what a woman poet could publish in English: epic ambition, social conscience, and some of the language’s most intimate love poetry.

works.title: How Do I Love Thee?
works.subtitle: How do I love thee? Let me count…

works.title: How Do I Love Thee?
works.subtitle: How do I love thee? Let me count…

works.title: How Do I Love Thee?
works.subtitle: How do I love thee? Let me count…

resonance: Her work models how private voice can carry public stakes without losing warmth.
quote: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.

name: Emily Dickinson
essence: Slant truth, hymn meters, and metaphysics in the size of a postage stamp.
bio: Emily Dickinson wrote with a telescope aimed inward: spare dashes, hymn stanzas, and riddles that open onto death, nature, and eternity. Her manuscripts still teach readers how much silence a poem can hold.

works.title: This is my letter to the world
works.subtitle: This is my letter to the world, That…

works.title: I'm nobody! Who are you?
works.subtitle: I'm nobody! Who are you? Are you nobody,…

works.title: Because I could not stop for Death
works.subtitle: Because I could not stop for Death, He…

resonance: Dickinson rewards rereading; each short poem is a door disguised as a wall.
quote: Tell all the truth but tell it slant—
quoteSource: Tell all the truth but tell it slant (1263)

name: Ezra Pound
essence: Image, economy, and translation as creative acts—modernism’s controversial engineer.
bio: Ezra Pound was a decisive editor and theorist of early modernist poetry, championing imagism and reshaping how English hears other traditions. His life and politics were deeply flawed; the archive includes him here for literary-historical context and selective reading.

works.title: In a Station of the Metro
works.subtitle: The apparition of these faces in the crowd;…

works.title: In a Station of the Metro
works.subtitle: The apparition of these faces in the crowd;…

works.title: In a Station of the Metro
works.subtitle: The apparition of these faces in the crowd;…

resonance: Engage Pound as you would any sharp tool: for craft lessons, with ethical distance intact.
quote: Go in fear of abstractions.

name: Hafez
essence: Wine, night, and the Beloved braided into ghazals that feel like breathing.
bio: Hafez (Shams al-Din Muhammad) is among the most beloved poets of the Persian language. His ghazals turn tavern, garden, and prayer into one luminous field where human longing meets divine presence—always through translation’s imperfect glass.

works.title: Pour the cup again I thought love would be easy
works.subtitle: Pour the cup again I thought love would…

works.title: Return and let courage enter this wounded heart
works.subtitle: Return and let courage enter this wounded heart…

works.title: My house became heaven when she lived in it
works.subtitle: My house became heaven when she lived in…

resonance: English renderings vary widely; Versery favors lines that keep music and mystery intact.
quote: I wish I could show you / when you are lonely or in darkness, / the astonishing light / of your own being.

name: H.D.
essence: Hard-edged images, classical myth, and the sea’s psychology pressed into line breaks.
bio: H.D. helped launch imagism—poetry of precise image and open rhythm—then moved into longer mythic sequences. Her work listens to antiquity as if it were a living shoreline.

works.title: Oread
works.subtitle: Whirl up, sea— whirl your pointed pines,…

works.title: Oread
works.subtitle: Whirl up, sea— whirl your pointed pines,…

works.title: Oread
works.subtitle: Whirl up, sea— whirl your pointed pines,…

resonance: H.D. teaches compression without explanation: the image does the thinking.
quote: O white face, / why do you lean over the salt sea?

name: James Weldon Johnson
essence: Anthem, sermon, and story—Black uplift voiced with lawyerly clarity and hymnody.
bio: James Weldon Johnson was a poet, diplomat, and NAACP leader who co-authored Lift Every Voice and Sing. His poems and essays braid civic hope with lyrical sorrow.

works.title: Lift Every Voice and Sing
works.subtitle: Lift every voice and sing, Till earth and…

works.title: Lift Every Voice and Sing
works.subtitle: Lift every voice and sing, Till earth and…

works.title: Lift Every Voice and Sing
works.subtitle: Lift every voice and sing, Till earth and…

resonance: Johnson’s public voice never forgets private feeling; dignity and ache share one breath.
quote: We have come over a way that with tears has been watered.

name: John Clare
essence: Field notebooks of a disappearing countryside—dialect, detail, and tender unease.
bio: John Clare wrote from deep inside rural England: birds, seasons, enclosure, and poverty recorded with almost folkloric fidelity. His work anticipates ecological attention long before the word existed.

works.title: I Am!
works.subtitle: I am! yet what I am none cares…

works.title: I Am!
works.subtitle: I am! yet what I am none cares…

works.title: I Am!
works.subtitle: I am! yet what I am none cares…

resonance: Clare reminds us that local names for things are a kind of love language.
quote: I am—yet what I am none cares or knows.

name: John Donne
essence: Passion stretched across theology and astronomy—wit as a form of devotion.
bio: John Donne remade English love poetry with conceits that snap shut like locks: body and soul, map and star, lover and compass. Dean of St. Paul’s, he also wrote sermons of thunderous compassion.

works.title: Holy Sonnet 10
works.subtitle: Death, be not proud, though some have called…

works.title: Holy Sonnet 10
works.subtitle: Death, be not proud, though some have called…

works.title: Holy Sonnet 10
works.subtitle: Death, be not proud, though some have called…

resonance: Donne rewards readers who enjoy being outpaced—then caught—by a metaphor.
quote: No man is an island, entire of itself.

name: John Keats
essence: Sensuous negative capability—beauty, mortality, and language at white heat.
bio: John Keats wrote for only a few years before tuberculosis ended his life, yet his odes reset English lyric pitch. His letters are as essential as his poems: a mind thinking in real time about art, suffering, and empathy.

works.title: When I have fears that I may cease to be
works.subtitle: When I have fears that I may cease…

works.title: On Seeing the Elgin Marbles
works.subtitle: My spirit is too weak—mortality Weighs heavily on…

works.title: To Autumn
works.subtitle: Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness, Close bosom-friend…

resonance: Keats teaches negative capability—the willingness to dwell in uncertainty without irritable reaching after fact.
quote: A thing of beauty is a joy for ever:

name: John Masefield
essence: Salt air, narrative verse, and the sea as moral weather.
bio: John Masefield served as UK Poet Laureate for decades; his ballads and sea poems brought narrative muscle back into popular poetry between wars.

works.title: Sea Fever
works.subtitle: I must go down to the seas again,…

works.title: Sea Fever
works.subtitle: I must go down to the seas again,…

works.title: Sea Fever
works.subtitle: I must go down to the seas again,…

resonance: Masefield’s archive presence is kinetic—motion, work, and weather in shared rhythm.
quote: I must go down to the seas again, to the lonely sea and the sky,

name: Kabir
essence: Plain-language paradox—Hindu and Muslim listeners addressed as one human knot.
bio: Kabir’s couplets cut through ritual pride toward a God beyond idols and quarrel. Attributed across centuries, his voice survives in oral lineage and countless translations—always partial, always provocative.

works.title: O servant, where dost thou seek Me?
works.subtitle: O servant, where dost thou seek Me? Lo!…

works.title: O brother, my heart yearns for that true Guru, who fills the cup
works.subtitle: O brother, my heart yearns for that true…

works.title: Where Spring, the lord of the seasons, reigneth, there the
works.subtitle: Where Spring, the lord of the seasons, reigneth,…

resonance: Kabir asks for sincerity over performance; the shortest line can overturn a habit.
quote: Are you looking for me? I am in the next seat.

name: Kahlil Gibran
essence: Parables in English prose-poetry—spiritual intimacy with a pamphlet’s reach.
bio: Kahlil Gibran emigrated from Ottoman Lebanon to Boston and New York, writing in Arabic and English. The Prophet became a global phenomenon; his shorter lyrics keep the same confessional glow.

works.title: On Love
works.subtitle: And he raised his head and looked upon…

works.title: On Freedom
works.subtitle: And he answered: At the city gate and…

works.title: The Path of the Night
works.subtitle: One may not reach the dawn save by…

resonance: Gibran’s sentences read like whispered advice that accidentally became scripture.
quote: Work is love made visible.

name: Langston Hughes
essence: Blues cadence in ink—Black American life with humor, ache, and civic pride.
bio: Langston Hughes made everyday Black speech a dignified poetic instrument. His jazz-age portraits, protest lyrics, and Montage of a Dream Deferred still anchor American literature.

works.title: Mother to Son
works.subtitle: Well, son, I'll tell you: Life for me…

works.title: Mother to Son
works.subtitle: Well, son, I'll tell you: Life for me…

works.title: Mother to Son
works.subtitle: Well, son, I'll tell you: Life for me…

resonance: Hughes proves that musicality is a form of truth-telling—not decoration.
quote: What happens to a dream deferred?

name: Lao Tzu
essence: Water-wisdom: softness overcoming hardness, names released, way as verb.
bio: The Dao De Jing, traditionally linked to Laozi, is a brief masterpiece of paradox and governance ethics. Its poems-as-chapters teach attention over assertion—always through translation’s many interpretations.

works.title: The Nameless
works.subtitle: The way you can explain is not the…

works.title: Return to the Root
works.subtitle: Make emptiness complete. Keep stillness steady.…

works.title: The Movement of the Way
works.subtitle: Returning is the motion of the Way. Yielding…

resonance: Versery excerpts foreground the poetic texture of philosophical lines—companions for reflection, not a course in comparative religion.
quote: Knowing others is intelligence; knowing yourself is true wisdom.

name: Mirza Ghalib
essence: Wit sharp as a dagger disguised as a rose—loss, wine, and metaphysics in couplets.
bio: Mirza Asadullah Khan Ghalib wrote Urdu and Persian ghazals that merge lover, skeptic, and mystic. His letters, equally celebrated, show a mind laughing at empire while mourning its ruins.

works.title: Before anything existed, there was God.
works.subtitle: Before anything existed, there was God. If nothing…

works.title: I rehearsed my death until it felt like craft.
works.subtitle: I rehearsed my death until it felt like…

works.title: It was never in my fate to meet you.
works.subtitle: It was never in my fate to meet…

resonance: Ghalib’s archive presence is all double meanings—read once for music, twice for sting.
quote: Each particle of dust sits crowned upon my head—

name: Rainer Maria Rilke
essence: Angels, inwardness, and the thing-poem—language tuned until it vibrates alone.
bio: Rainer Maria Rilke moved between Prague, Paris, and Swiss solitude, writing the Duino Elegies and Sonnets to Orpheus. His letters to young poets remain a secular scripture on patience and perception.

works.title: Autumn Day
works.subtitle: Lord it is time. The summer was immense.…

works.title: Love Song
works.subtitle: How shall I hold my soul so that…

works.title: Sonnets to Orpheus I, 5
works.subtitle: Set up no monument. Let the rose bloom…

resonance: Rilke asks us to love the questions themselves—then live slowly enough to hear answers form.
quote: You must change your life.
quoteSource: Archaic Torso of Apollo (Stephen Mitchell translation tradition)

name: Ralph Waldo Emerson
essence: Self-reliance as spiritual practice—nature, intuition, and democratic awe.
bio: Ralph Waldo Emerson helped midwife American intellectual independence. His essays and poems braid Unitarian sermon with Romantic nature mysticism.

works.title: Brahma
works.subtitle: If the red slayer think he slays, Or…

works.title: Brahma
works.subtitle: If the red slayer think he slays, Or…

works.title: Brahma
works.subtitle: If the red slayer think he slays, Or…

resonance: Emerson rewards readers building an inner compass—less doctrine, more invitation.
quote: Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string.

name: Robert Frost
essence: New England roads, fences, and snow—plain talk hiding ethical crossroads.
bio: Robert Frost won four Pulitzers and a nation’s ear for blank verse that sounds like conversation—until the conversation turns uncanny. Farm walls and forest paths become metaphysics.

works.title: The Road Not Taken
works.subtitle: Two roads diverged in a yellow wood, And…

works.title: For Once, Then, Something
works.subtitle: Others taunt me with having knelt at well-curbs…

works.title: Gathering Leaves
works.subtitle: Spades take up leaves No better than spoons,…

resonance: Frost’s best poems turn after the last line; the reader becomes the traveler who chose.
quote: Two roads diverged in a wood, and I— / I took the one less traveled by,

name: Robert Herrick
essence: Carpe florem—small joys, brief beauty, and wit polished like silver.
bio: Robert Herrick’s Hesperides gathers hundreds of short lyrics: to Julia, to dew, to fleeting time. A country priest’s book that drinks deep of pagan delight.

works.title: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
works.subtitle: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may, Old Time…

works.title: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
works.subtitle: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may, Old Time…

works.title: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
works.subtitle: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may, Old Time…

resonance: Herrick teaches lightness as discipline—serious craft in praise of unserious moments.
quote: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may, / Old Time is still a-flying:
quoteSource: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time

name: Rudyard Kipling
essence: Barrack-room ballads, jungle law, and the empire’s conscience in rough rhyme.
bio: Rudyard Kipling was born in Bombay, educated in England, and returned to India as a journalist. His verse ranges from soldierly anecdote to children’s myth; read today with historical awareness of imperial power.

works.title: Cities and Thrones and Powers
works.subtitle: _Cities and Thrones and Powers, Stand in Time's…

works.title: A Charm
works.subtitle: Take of English earth as much As either…

works.title: If—
works.subtitle: If you can keep your head when all…

resonance: Kipling’s technical gifts are undeniable; his politics require critical framing—both can coexist in study.
quote: If you can keep your head when all about you / Are losing theirs and blaming it on you,

name: Ryokan
essence: Humility, haikai spirit, and the joke that enlightenment might look like doing chores badly.
bio: Ryōkan Taigu was a Sōtō Zen monk whose poems and calligraphy blend childlike simplicity with depth. He favored mendicancy over temple politics; his ink still feels like breathing room.

works.title: When thoughts run out
works.subtitle: When thoughts run out I step into the…

works.title: Spring will open every branch
works.subtitle: Spring will open every branch. Flowers will return.…

works.title: When I was young
works.subtitle: When I was young I put books aside…

resonance: Ryokan’s lines model how humor and holiness can share one tatami mat.
quote: Too lazy to be ambitious, / I let the world take care of itself.

name: Sarojini Naidu
essence: Nightingale of India—song woven with congress speeches and feminist courage.
bio: Sarojini Naidu was a poet, orator, and independence leader. Her English lyrics draw on Indian landscapes and folk forms while addressing modern nationhood and women’s dignity.

works.title: Coromandel Fishers
works.subtitle: Rise, brothers, rise; the wakening skies pray to…

works.title: Coromandel Fishers
works.subtitle: Rise, brothers, rise; the wakening skies pray to…

works.title: Coromandel Fishers
works.subtitle: Rise, brothers, rise; the wakening skies pray to…

resonance: Naidu shows how public speech and private music can strengthen each other.
quote: We shall remember when loveliness / Walks eager through the Door of Life / Liptinted with delirious bliss / In rosegold and in rose.

name: Siegfried Sassoon
essence: Trench truth in controlled anger—satire and pity sharing one gasp.
bio: Siegfried Sassoon’s war poems record moral shock in plain English. Soldier, protester, memoirist, he helped shape how literature remembers 1914–1918.

works.title: Suicide in the Trenches
works.subtitle: I knew a simple soldier boy Who grinned…

works.title: Suicide in the Trenches
works.subtitle: I knew a simple soldier boy Who grinned…

works.title: Suicide in the Trenches
works.subtitle: I knew a simple soldier boy Who grinned…

resonance: Sassoon proves understatement can carry fury—no fireworks, only scar tissue.
quote: Does it matter?—losing your sight? / There’s such splendid work for the blind;

name: T. S. Eliot
essence: Fragment as method—city prayer, mythic collage, and the hollow men’s echo.
bio: T. S. Eliot reshaped English poetry with The Waste Land and Four Quartets, and shaped taste through criticism. British citizen, American roots, Anglican convert—his work is as contested as it is canonical.

works.title: Gerontion
works.subtitle: Thou hast nor youth nor age But as…

works.title: Whispers of Immortality
works.subtitle: Webster was much possessed by death And saw…

works.title: Rhapsody on a Windy Night
works.subtitle: Twelve o'clock. Along the reaches of the street…

resonance: Eliot rewards slow excavation—every allusion is a door, not a lock.
quote: This is the way the world ends / Not with a bang but a whimper.

name: Rabindranath Tagore
essence: Song-offerings: nature, devotion, and freedom pressed into lyric sequence.
bio: Rabindranath Tagore wrote novels, essays, music, and poetry that helped define modern Bengali culture; Gitanjali won the Nobel Prize in Literature. His English renderings opened a global readership—always a glimpse, never the whole ocean.

works.title: I ask for a moment’s indulgence to sit
works.subtitle: I ask for a moment’s indulgence to sit…

works.title: If thou speakest not I will fill my
works.subtitle: If thou speakest not I will fill my…

works.title: The morning sea of silence broke into ripples
works.subtitle: The morning sea of silence broke into ripples…

resonance: Tagore’s archive presence is gentle light—invitation to widen the heart’s vocabulary.
quote: The same stream of life that runs through my veins night and day / runs through the world and dances in rhythmic measures.

name: Thomas Nashe
essence: Pamphlet energy, plague-year vision, and prose that snaps like a street cry.
bio: Thomas Nashe wrote plays, satires, and fantastical prose in the fierce print culture of Elizabethan London. His verse can feel like a torch carried too close to the face—glaring, comic, uncanny.

works.title: A Litany in Time of Plague
works.subtitle: Adieu, farewell earth's bliss, This world uncertain is,…

works.title: A Litany in Time of Plague
works.subtitle: Adieu, farewell earth's bliss, This world uncertain is,…

works.title: A Litany in Time of Plague
works.subtitle: Adieu, farewell earth's bliss, This world uncertain is,…

resonance: Nashe reminds us that early modern English was a playground as much as a temple.
quote: Brightness falls from the air; / Queens have died young and fair;

name: Wallace Stevens
essence: Florida light in metaphysics—insurance executive by day, supreme fiction by night.
bio: Wallace Stevens spent his career in surety law while writing some of American poetry’s most luxurious meditations on imagination, weather, and belief without God.

works.title: Anecdote of the Jar
works.subtitle: I placed a jar in Tennessee, And round…

works.title: Anecdote of the Jar
works.subtitle: I placed a jar in Tennessee, And round…

works.title: Anecdote of the Jar
works.subtitle: I placed a jar in Tennessee, And round…

resonance: Stevens asks whether the mind’s fiction is the only sacred ground left—and answers with music.
quote: I do not know which to prefer, / The beauty of inflections / Or the beauty of innuendoes, / The blackbird whistling / Or just after.
quoteSource: Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird

name: William Carlos Williams
essence: No ideas but in things—red wheelbarrow modernity, doctor’s-eye clarity.
bio: William Carlos Williams practiced medicine in New Jersey while writing poems that insisted the local was universal. Paterson and the short imagist lyrics still reset poetic attention.

works.title: The Red Wheelbarrow

works.title: The Red Wheelbarrow

works.title: The Red Wheelbarrow
works.subtitle: so much depends upon…

resonance: Williams teaches seeing before judging—the poem as diagnosis of attention.
quote: so much depends / upon / a red wheel / barrow

name: Anonymous (Wisdom Literature / Modernized)
essence: Existential steel, cosmic scale, and practical wisdom rendered in modern free verse.
bio: A curated modernized rendering from the King James tradition focused on Ecclesiastes, Job, Psalms, Proverbs, and Song of Solomon. These passages retain ancient imagery and philosophical weight while removing archaic syntax for contemporary readability.

works.title: Chasing the Wind

works.title: Iron Sharpens Iron
works.subtitle: Iron sharpens iron. One person…

works.title: Trust with All Your Heart
works.subtitle: My son, do not forget my teaching; let…

resonance: This voice holds both dust and firmament—stark counsel for grief, wonder, and grounded living.
quote: Smoke of smoke—everything is smoke.
